breakClass: left-join null member access / non-nullable value-column read on a possibly-null DefaultIfEmpty() entity in a server-side projection (EF Core 10 shaper reads SQL NULL into a non-nullable CLR value type â€” decimal/int/DateTime/enum â€” and throws; EF6 silently produced default(T))
why: Same grid pattern as PurchaseReturnController â€” PRPayments left join empty -> non-nullable Status/PReturnAmount read NULL -> 500. Legacy identical.
finalFix:
Guard the three NULL-c reads in the server projection so EF Core 10 emits NULL-tolerant SQL (matches EF6's default(T) behavior). In GetPurchaseReturn, change lines 862/864/866 from:
    PaymentStatus = c.Status,
    ...
    c.PReturnAmount,
    ...
    BalanceAmt = a.PRGrandTotal - c.PReturnAmount,
to:
    PaymentStatus = c != null ? c.Status : 0,
    ...
    PReturnAmount = c != null ? c.PReturnAmount : 0m,
    ...
    BalanceAmt = a.PRGrandTotal - (c != null ? c.PReturnAmount : 0m),
Note: line 864 uses the shorthand member-name form `c.PReturnAmount`, so the guarded version must give it an explicit name `PReturnAmount = ...` (the anonymous-type member name stays "PReturnAmount", so downstream serverRows.Select(o => o.PReturnAmount) at line 930 is unaffected). The `c != null` translates to the SQL coalescing/CASE that EF Core 10 supports across the DefaultIfEmpty, and 0 / 0m reproduce default(int)/default(decimal) exactly as EF6 produced them â€” output-preserving for the only realistic value (an orphaned/unpaid Debit Note shows status 0, returned amount 0, balance = full grand total). The (decimal?) casts already present in the WHERE clause need no change.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED (medium severity â€” edge data only). File: QuickNetCore/Controllers/PurchaseReturnNoteController.cs, method GetPurchaseReturn (def line 742), projection lines 862 (PaymentStatus = c.Status), 864 (c.PReturnAmount), 866 (BalanceAmt = a.PRGrandTotal - c.PReturnAmount).

(a) SERVER-SIDE: confirmed. These fields are projected inside `serverQuery` (composed lines 824-875) which is first enumerated by `serverQuery.ToList()` at line 887. There is NO .ToList()/.AsEnumerable() boundary before the projection, so EF Core 10 translates this `select new { ... }` to SQL. (The later serverRows.Select(o=>...) at line 911 is client-side and merely copies the already-materialized o.PaymentStatus/o.PReturnAmount/o.BalanceAmt, so it adds no risk and is irrelevant.) `c` is a LEFT JOIN: `join c in db.PRPayments ... into pay from c in pay.DefaultIfEmpty()` (lines 827-828).

(b) WOULD 500: confirmed on edge data. Entity PRPayment (Models/PurchaseReturn.cs:132) declares `decimal PReturnAmount` (line 147) and `int Status` (line 153) â€” both NON-NULLABLE value types. When a PurchaseReturn has no matching PRPayment, DefaultIfEmpty emits SQL NULL for all c.* columns. EF Core 10's shaper reads SQL NULL into non-nullable decimal/int and throws InvalidOperationException (->500); EF6 silently yielded default(T). The `Balance == null` short-circuit at line 845 (`(Balance == null) || ...`) does NOT exclude NULL-c rows, so an orphaned Debit Note reaches the shaper. The (decimal?) casts on line 845 only protect the WHERE filter, never the SELECT projection.

PORT REGRESSION: confirmed. Legacy _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/PurchaseReturnNoteController.cs is byte-for-byte equivalent: DefaultIfEmpty join (line 843), PaymentStatus = c.Status (880), c.PReturnAmount (882), BalanceAmt = a.PRGrandTotal - c.PReturnAmount (884), same Balance short-circuit (863). It worked under EF6 only because EF6 materialized NULL value-columns to default(T).

SEVERITY = MEDIUM (not high). Normal create always inserts a matching PRPayment (line 562, after PurchaseReturn at 336). So on well-formed data c is never NULL and the query succeeds. A NULL c arises only from ORPHANED data â€” and that is genuinely possible because Create saves the PurchaseReturn (SaveChanges line 337) and the PRPayment (SaveChanges line 563) in TWO SEPARATE non-transactional saves with no BeginTransaction wrapping them, so a failure/crash between them leaves a Debit Note with no PRPayment; independent deletion/migration can also orphan it. Thus it will not 500 on typical day-to-day data, but WILL 500 deterministically whenever a PRPayment-less Debit Note falls in range. The hunter's substantive claim is correct; only the implied "realistic data" framing is too strong.
